summary:
The Battle of Adrianople (August 9, 378 AD) was one of the most catastrophic military defeats in Roman history — a disastrous engagement in which the Eastern Roman Emperor Valens was killed along with an estimated two-thirds of the Eastern Roman army (perhaps 20,000–30,000 men), shattered by a Visigothic and Ostrogothic force that the empire had itself invited across the Danube. The crisis began in 376 AD when the Visigoths, fleeing the [[event:2106|Hunnic invasions]], crossed the Danube as refugees seeking Roman protection. Corrupt Roman officials exploited and abused the refugee population, driving them to revolt; when Valens marched to suppress the uprising, he attacked before his western colleague Gratian could reinforce him.

The Gothic cavalry — including a charge by Ostrogothic horsemen who arrived late in the battle — overwhelmed the Roman infantry, cutting it to pieces. Valens's body was never found. Adrianople's consequences were profound: for the first time, a large barbarian force was allowed to settle inside Roman territory as a self-governing group (foederati) rather than being assimilated into Roman society. This created a precedent — a Germanic people within the empire's borders maintaining their own identity, laws, and leadership — that would be repeated across the Western Empire and that fundamentally undermined the logic of Roman assimilation. Military historians have called Adrianople the beginning of the end for the Western Empire and a turning point in the history of ancient warfare, as Gothic heavy cavalry demonstrated the vulnerability of Roman legionary formations.
